---
title: "exec"
---

`exec` is the universal primitive: it runs C# inside the live Editor and returns
a value. Everything else — scene inspection, entity queries, mutation — is `exec`
specialized. Because it is universal, its discipline matters most.

## Totality: always return

An `exec` that does not return tells you nothing, and "nothing" is
indistinguishable from "silently failed." The behavior layer marks any `exec`
block without a `return` as **partial** and warns at render time. Make every
`exec` total.

```{.exec name="Count cameras"}
return UnityEngine.Object.FindObjectsOfType<UnityEngine.Camera>().Length;
```

## stdin for anything non-trivial

Trivial reads can go inline. Anything with quotes, interpolation, generics,
lambdas, namespaces, LINQ, or multiple statements goes through a named heredoc
on stdin. The delimiter is `'CSHARP'`, quoted, so bash does not expand the body.

```{.cli name="The heredoc form"}
cat << 'CSHARP' | unity-cli exec
var go = new UnityEngine.GameObject("Marker");
return go.name;
CSHARP
```

Piped code does not support `using` statements in the body. Bring namespaces in
with the `--usings` flag instead.

```{.exec name="Query with explicit usings" usings="Unity.Entities,Unity.Mathematics"}
var em = World.DefaultGameObjectInjectionWorld.EntityManager;
var q = em.CreateEntityQuery(typeof(LocalTransform));
return "entities with LocalTransform: " + q.CalculateEntityCount();
```

## while over foreach

`foreach` works in `exec` until it does not, and when it fails the error is
opaque. The codex uses `while` with an explicit index everywhere it iterates,
because the deterministic form always parses and reads the same way.

```{.exec name="Enumerate roots deterministically"}
var roots = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.GetActiveScene().GetRootGameObjects();
var sb = new System.Text.StringBuilder();
var i = 0;
while (i < roots.Length) {
    sb.AppendLine($"[{i}] {roots[i].name} (children: {roots[i].transform.childCount})");
    i++;
}
return sb.ToString();
```

## Assembly visibility

If `exec` fails with a `TypeLoadException` for a type you know exists, the
assembly is not loaded or is gated behind a define constraint. Inspect the
`asmdef` for `defineConstraints`, confirm the assembly is built, and force a
recompile.

```{.cli name="Force the assembly to load"}
unity-cli editor refresh --force --compile
```

::: {.gotcha title="Refresh may return before it is done"}
A compile-triggering refresh can close the connection before responding. That
is not success and not failure — it is inconclusive. Reconnect with a minimal
total `exec` and read the compile state directly.
:::

```{.exec name="Reconnect probe"}
return 42;
```

If that returns `42`, the connection is back and compilation has settled enough
to evaluate. Now re-run your real query.
